reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware mechanistic reasoning about how flame structure (diffusion vs partially premixed, residence/mixing time) constrains admissible claims about CO/CO2 and an intervention, while explicitly penalizing common overreach moves (material-property substitution, SOC-based extrapolation, ratio-to-mass confusion). Decoys are mostly single-fault and engineering-plausible, though two hinge on generic overgeneralization patterns that may be quickly dismissed by experts, slightly limiting discriminative separation at the top end.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware evaluation of a multi-claim safety argument using limited SOC-dependent fire/explosion observations, rewarding partial defensibility while penalizing system-level and causality overreach. Decoys are engineering-plausible and mostly single-fault (runaway-prevention inference; propagation guarantee), producing meaningful expert discrimination. Minor ambiguity remains because some “supported by no explosion observed” language can be read as too strong a generalization beyond the stated test conditions.

The prompt tightly focuses on a single mechanistic axis: how a diffusion-to-partially-premixed flame transition (via mixing/flow time) explains both CO/CO2 shift and the coupled particle/explosion observations across SOC. The item has strong discriminative power because it forces integration of three observables (ratio, particle presence, explosion timing) without inviting ratio-to-mass or chemistry-generalization extrapolations. Minor weakness is that as a short-answer item, it lacks explicit decoy options, so the purity/diversity of wrong-answer failure modes is less controllable.
